claim: The dominant word-initial glyph constraints {o, c, q, s, d} and word-final constraints {y, n, l, r, o} are artefacts of a Vigenere-like polyalphabetic cipher with a key length of 3-7 characters, such that glyph positional bias within words is a function of key-phase position rather than the underlying language's phonotactics, and the index of coincidence computed within each key-phase position would be elevated (above 0.065) relative to the overall corpus index of coincidence.
evidence_for: Strong positional glyph constraints are difficult to explain by natural language phonotactics alone and may reflect cipher key cycling
Glyph entropy (3.8627 bits) is slightly below Latin (~4.0), consistent with modest index-of-coincidence elevation from a polyalphabetic cipher with short key
Average word length 5.03 is consistent with a key length in the 3-7 range producing detectable periodic patterns
evidence_against: Vigenere-type ciphers typically do not produce strong word-boundary-aligned positional constraints unless key resets at word boundaries â€” an unusual design
Existing high-confidence beliefs (0.946, 0.863) favor word-boundary constraints as reflecting cipher structure or Vigenere-like word-boundary key resets rather than intra-word periodicity
If key resets at each word start, a Kasiski/Friedman test on the full character stream would fail to detect period, making the test harder to apply
test: Apply Friedman index-of-coincidence test across the full linearized glyph sequence (ignoring word boundaries) using sliding windows of length 3 through 10; additionally, if word-boundary key reset is suspected, compute index of coincidence within each word-position stratum (position 1, 2, 3, ...) across all words of length >= 5 and test whether any stratum shows IC above 0.065.
test_metric: Index of coincidence for each candidate key length (3-10) in Friedman test; per-position IC across words stratified by within-word glyph position
test_threshold: Confirm polyalphabetic cipher with key length k if IC at period k exceeds 0.065 (Latin IC ~0.0667) and is significantly above overall corpus IC; deny if no period in 3-10 shows IC elevation and per-position IC is uniform across word positions

reasoning: A polyalphabetic explanation for positional constraints would be confirmed or eliminated by IC periodicity analysis, which is computationally straightforward; if denied, it shifts probability mass toward the word-boundary cipher-reset model already supported by existing high-confidence hypotheses.
